questions.q: What does the abbreviation EVM stand for in Indian elections?
questions.explanation: EVM stands for Electronic Voting Machine, the device used to record votes in Indian elections.

questions.q: What did EVMs replace in the conduct of Indian elections?
questions.explanation: EVMs replaced the earlier system of paper ballots used to record votes.

questions.q: In which year was an EVM first used in an Indian election?
questions.explanation: An EVM was first used in a 1982 by-election in Kerala.

questions.q: In which state was the EVM first used in 1982?
questions.explanation: The EVM was first used in a 1982 by-election in the Paravur assembly constituency in Kerala.

questions.q: By which general election were EVMs adopted nationwide across all constituencies?
questions.explanation: EVMs were adopted nationwide for the general elections of 2004.

questions.q: EVMs are paired with which printed-record unit in Indian elections?
questions.explanation: EVMs are paired with VVPAT units that produce a paper record of each vote.

questions.q: Which body administers the use of EVMs in Indian elections?
questions.explanation: The Election Commission of India administers elections and the use of EVMs.

questions.q: What does the abbreviation VVPAT stand for?
questions.explanation: VVPAT stands for Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail.

questions.q: An EVM in India typically consists of two connected units. What are they?
questions.explanation: An EVM consists of a Control Unit operated by polling staff and a Balloting Unit used by voters.

questions.q: Which unit of the EVM does the voter use to cast a vote?
questions.explanation: The voter presses a button on the Balloting Unit to cast a vote.

questions.q: Which two public-sector undertakings manufacture EVMs used in Indian elections?
options: Hindustan Aeronautics Limited and Steel Authority of India
Bharat Electronics Limited and Electronics Corporation of India Limited
Bharat Heavy Electricals and Gail India
Indian Telephone Industries and Coal India
questions.explanation: EVMs are manufactured by Bharat Electronics Limited (BEL) and Electronics Corporation of India Limited (ECIL).

questions.q: The unit of the EVM operated by polling officials to release each ballot is the:
questions.explanation: The Control Unit is operated by polling officials, who release a ballot for each voter.

questions.q: What does the VVPAT unit produce when a voter casts a vote?
options: A digital receipt sent to the voter's phone
A printed paper slip showing the chosen candidate
questions.explanation: The VVPAT prints a paper slip showing the chosen candidate and symbol, briefly visible to the voter.

questions.q: The NOTA option, allowing a voter to reject all candidates, was introduced on EVMs following a 2013 ruling by which court?
questions.explanation: Following a 2013 Supreme Court ruling, the None of the Above (NOTA) option was added to EVMs.

questions.q: What does the NOTA button on an EVM allow a voter to do?
questions.explanation: NOTA, None of the Above, lets a voter formally choose not to support any of the listed candidates.

questions.q: Indian EVMs are designed as standalone devices, meaning they are:
options: Linked to a central online server
Not connected to any network or the internet
questions.explanation: Indian EVMs are standalone machines not connected to any network or the internet.

questions.q: Indian EVMs are powered primarily by which source, allowing use without continuous mains electricity?
questions.explanation: EVMs run on an internal battery, enabling their use even where mains power is unavailable.

questions.q: Compared with paper ballots, one practical advantage cited for EVMs is faster:
questions.explanation: EVMs allow much faster counting of votes than manual counting of paper ballots.

questions.q: In the conduct of polls, EVMs are kept secure between polling and counting in:
questions.explanation: After polling, EVMs are sealed and stored in guarded strong rooms until counting day.

questions.q: The maximum number of candidates a single Balloting Unit can accommodate is generally limited; additional units are linked when there are more candidates than:
questions.explanation: When candidates exceed a single Balloting Unit's capacity, additional units are connected to the Control Unit.

questions.q: Each EVM Balloting Unit lists candidates along with their names and which other identifier?
questions.explanation: The Balloting Unit displays each candidate's name and allotted election symbol.

questions.q: EVMs were used in Indian elections for the first time in 1982 under powers exercised by which authority, before later statutory backing?
questions.explanation: The Election Commission introduced EVMs in 1982 in a by-election before later legislative backing for their use.

questions.q: The 1982 by-election where EVMs were first used was in which assembly constituency of Kerala?
questions.explanation: The EVM was first used in the Paravur (Parur) assembly constituency by-election in Kerala in 1982.

questions.q: Use of EVMs was given clearer legal footing by an amendment to which law in 1989?
options: The Representation of the People Act, 1951
questions.explanation: A 1989 amendment to the Representation of the People Act, 1951 provided legal backing for the use of voting machines.

questions.q: In which year did the Supreme Court direct that VVPAT be introduced alongside EVMs?
questions.explanation: In 2013 the Supreme Court directed the introduction of VVPAT to enhance transparency in EVM-based voting.

questions.q: EVMs were first used on a limited basis in a general (Lok Sabha) election in which year, before full nationwide adoption?
questions.explanation: EVMs were used in selected constituencies in the 1999 general election before nationwide use in 2004.

questions.q: The general election of 2004 was significant for EVMs because it was the first Lok Sabha election in which they were used:
options: In every constituency across the country
questions.explanation: The 2004 general election was the first in which EVMs were used in every parliamentary constituency.

questions.q: VVPAT slips are stored in a sealed compartment so that they can be used for:
questions.explanation: VVPAT slips drop into a sealed box and can be counted to verify EVM results when required.

questions.q: Which feature of the Indian EVM design is intended to prevent rapid repeated voting by limiting the rate of recorded votes?
options: A fingerprint scanner on every unit
A mandatory two-minute lockout for all voters
A built-in restriction on votes per unit time
questions.explanation: Indian EVMs are designed to record only a limited number of votes per minute, limiting the rate of voting.

questions.q: Once a vote is recorded on an EVM, the machine is designed so that the Control Unit:
options: Allows the voter to vote again
Displays the running tally to the public
Transmits the vote to a server
Locks until the next ballot is released
questions.explanation: After a vote is cast, the EVM locks so no further vote can be entered until officials release the next ballot.
